Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after work and on weekends when I could carve out time. Being a kinesthetic learner, I'd often pace around my small apartment while reviewing notes or use flashcards I could physically manipulate. With limited resources, I had to be creative about when and where I could study effectively. I aimed for about 20 hours a week, though some weeks were definitely harder than others to maintain that.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I tried to keep some regularity, like Sunday afternoons being dedicated study time, but deadlines would throw everything off. When assignments were coming up, I'd cram more hours in, and during quieter weeks I might slack a bit. The anxiety of falling behind would sometimes push me to study at odd hours just to feel like I was keeping up.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there were new announcements or seeing if feedback had been posted on my work. I'd also log in when I needed to watch lecture videos or participate in discussions with my peers. Sometimes I'd just log in out of nervous habit, honestly, to make sure I hadn't missed something important. The peer support group I was part of would often prompt me to check in too when someone posted a question.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. I'd do it during my lunch break at work or first thing when I got home. Having peers who were active in discussions made me want to stay connected and not miss out on helpful conversations. It felt less like a chore and more like checking in with a study community.
Q5: The discussion forums and peer interactions were honestly lifesavers for me. Being able to talk through concepts with other students helped things click in a way that just reading never did. The videos were useful too, especially when I could pause and take notes or rewind parts I didn't understand. I learn best by doing and discussing, so anything interactive felt more valuable than passive reading.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the longer reading materials or supplementary PDFs. With limited internet at times and no printer access, downloading and reading dense documents felt overwhelming. Some of the optional quizzes I'd put off too, telling myself I'd get to them later but never quite managing it. If something didn't feel immediately connected to an assignment, it was easy to let it slide.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Peer discussions felt valuable because I'd often walk away with new perspectives or study tips. If I could physically engage with something or apply it practically, that made it feel worthwhile. Abstract theoretical readings without clear application felt like a waste of my limited time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford to indulge in, honestly. Required ones had this weight of consequence attached to them, so I'd prioritize those even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff I'd save for 'when I have time,' which rarely happened. Sometimes I resented that optional activities might have actually helped me more but I couldn't justify spending time on them over required work.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos when I had good wifi and watch them later without internet. I made physical flashcards and study sheets that I'd carry around with me everywhere. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and studied from those at home. A lot of my learning happened through hands-on practice problems I'd work through on paper.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be at my kitchen table working through problems by hand or reviewing printed notes, and none of that showed up anywhere online. When I'd meet up with peers from my support group in person to study together, that was some of my most productive learning time. I'd also record myself explaining concepts and listen back to them during my commute. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study effort.
Q11: All the time, yes. I'd download a video or PDF once, then study from it for days or weeks without logging back in. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned early on to grab materials when I could and work with them offline. I had folders on my laptop organized by week with everything I needed. It was more efficient for me than constantly streaming or re-accessing things online.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My stress levels were through the roof, and my anxiety would spike to the point where even opening the LMS felt overwhelming. Financial pressures and work demands competed for my mental energy. During those times, I'd still try to engage but it felt like I was just going through the motions rather than really learning.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, I'd log in but just kind of stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other necessities. Confusion about certain topics would make me avoid those modules, which only made things worse. Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy – I'd fall asleep watching lecture videos more than I'd like to admit.
Q14: Oh yes, there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or work issues. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. Sometimes I'd open materials with good intentions but realize twenty minutes later I hadn't retained a single thing. It's like my body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments that felt too complex or that I didn't fully understand. The anxiety of potentially failing made me avoid starting things, which is completely counterproductive, I know. Sometimes I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better before attempting an assignment, then never feel ready enough. Fear of confirming I wasn't smart enough kept me from trying sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system since my internal motivation was pretty low. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage intensely with the LMS. My activity probably looked very spiky – quiet periods followed by frantic bursts of logging in and submitting work. Without deadlines, I honestly might not have engaged as much as I did.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me when I actually received it. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me want to engage more with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to submit future work. I appreciated detailed comments that showed me how to improve rather than just a grade. The timing mattered too – feedback that came quickly felt more useful.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me know what to expect and plan around my work schedule. Having clear modules with defined activities made it easier to break things into manageable chunks. The peer support component was huge for me – knowing I had people to discuss things with made the course feel less isolating. When the structure was clear, my anxiety was more manageable.
Q19: The discussion forums and peer interaction features made engagement easier because they played to my strengths as someone who learns through doing and talking. The flexibility to access materials at different times worked well with my unpredictable schedule. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when I couldn't figure out what was actually required versus optional. Dense text-heavy content without interactive elements was always a struggle for me.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions, but then struggling to actually complete and submit polished assignments. High anxiety can make you engage with materials constantly while also making it terrifying to submit work that might be judged. Sometimes understanding the content and producing formal academic work are two very different skills. You might be learning a lot but not translating it into submitted work.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much learning can happen offline. Someone might download everything in one session, study intensively from those materials, and perform well on exams without logging in much. Or they might have strong prior knowledge and only need minimal engagement with new materials. Efficiency looks different for different learners – some people don't need to watch every video three times like I do.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you're clicking on, which gives some insight into habits and engagement timing. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement – whether you're actually thinking or just scrolling. They miss all offline studying, peer conversations that happen outside the platform, and the emotional experience of learning. They can't capture whether someone is struggling with anxiety while staring at a screen or fully absorbed in learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, connecting ideas to things I already know or to real life. For me, it involves discussing with others and trying to apply concepts practically. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent looking at a screen. When I'm truly engaged, time flies and I feel energized rather than drained.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support group meant to me emotionally and academically, especially the conversations we had outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my anxiety levels or how much courage it took some days just to submit an assignment. All my offline studying – the flashcards, the borrowed textbooks, the voice recordings – none of that appears anywhere. And it definitely can't show that despite my struggles, I was genuinely happy to be learning and growing, even when it was hard.
